Reason (R): In a sub-division, each share of higher denomination is split into multiple shares of lower denomination, creating more shares with proportionately lower face value.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is FALSE — sub-division increases the NUMBER of shares but does NOT change total Share Capital. Each share splits into smaller shares of lower face value, but the total capital (number × face value) remains constant. E.g., 1,000 shares × ₹100 = ₹1,00,000; after sub-division: 10,000 shares × ₹10 = ₹1,00,000 (unchanged). Reason R is TRUE — R correctly describes the mechanism of sub-division (splitting shares of higher denomination into multiple shares of lower denomination). R is accurate, but A incorrectly claims total capital increases. Hence A is false, R is true.

questions.question: Lyra Ltd. has Equity Share Capital of ₹25,00,000 (2,50,000 shares × ₹10). The company proposes a rights issue of 1 share for every 5 held at ₹14 each. All existing shareholders subscribe. What is the new Equity Share Capital and Securities Premium after the rights issue?
options: Share Capital ₹30,00,000; SPR ₹2,00,000
Share Capital ₹35,00,000; SPR ₹4,00,000
Share Capital ₹30,00,000; SPR ₹2,40,000
Share Capital ₹25,00,000; SPR ₹2,00,000
questions.explanation: Rights issue: 1 for 5 → New shares = 2,50,000/5 = 50,000 shares at ₹14. New Share Capital = 50,000 × ₹10 = ₹5,00,000. New SPR = 50,000 × (₹14 − ₹10) = 50,000 × ₹4 = ₹2,00,000. Post-issue Share Capital = ₹25,00,000 + ₹5,00,000 = ₹30,00,000. Post-issue SPR = ₹0 + ₹2,00,000 = ₹2,00,000 (assuming no prior SPR). Total new equity Share Capital = ₹30,00,000; SPR = ₹2,00,000. Cash received from rights issue = 50,000 × ₹14 = ₹7,00,000. Answer: Option A.

questions.question: What does 'Equity' represent in the accounting equation 'Assets = Liabilities + Equity'?
options: Total borrowed funds of the company
Residual interest of the owners in the assets after deducting all liabilities
Total fixed assets of the company
Current market value of company's shares
questions.explanation: In accounting, Equity (or Owners' Equity/Shareholders' Funds) represents the RESIDUAL INTEREST — what belongs to the owners after all liabilities (obligations to creditors) have been settled. Equity = Total Assets − Total Liabilities. It includes Share Capital + Reserves and Surplus (retained earnings). This is why shareholders are called residual claimants — they own what's left after all debts are paid. Option A describes liabilities (borrowed funds). Option C describes a specific class of assets. Option D refers to market data irrelevant to accounting equity.

questions.question: Norma Ltd.'s Balance Sheet shows: Equity Share Capital ₹40,00,000; 10% Preference Share Capital ₹10,00,000; Securities Premium ₹5,00,000; General Reserve ₹8,00,000; P&L Deficit (Dr balance) ₹(3,00,000). What are the total Shareholders' Funds?
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Share Capital + Reserves and Surplus. Share Capital = ₹40,00,000 + ₹10,00,000 = ₹50,00,000. Reserves and Surplus = SPR (₹5,00,000) + General Reserve (₹8,00,000) + P&L Deficit (−₹3,00,000) = ₹10,00,000. Total Shareholders' Funds = ₹50,00,000 + ₹10,00,000 = ₹60,00,000. The P&L deficit is shown as a NEGATIVE figure under Reserves and Surplus, reducing it. This is a critical balance sheet presentation concept — losses reduce shareholders' funds. The debit balance in P&L Account = ₹3,00,000 is subtracted from positive reserves.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of preference shares?
options: Fixed rate of dividend specified at the time of issue
Dividend must be paid every year regardless of profits
Priority over equity shares in payment of dividend
Priority over equity shares in repayment of capital at winding up
questions.explanation: Option B is NOT a characteristic of preference shares — it is actually a MISCONCEPTION. Preference dividend does NOT have to be paid every year regardless of profits. Preference dividends are: (a) Only paid when the company has sufficient distributable profits AND the Board declares a dividend; (b) For non-cumulative preference shares, if profits are insufficient in a year, the right to that year's dividend is permanently lost. Even for cumulative preference shares, the dividend accumulates but is still only paid from future profits. Options A (fixed rate), C (priority in dividend), and D (priority in capital repayment) are genuine features.

questions.question: Sculptor Ltd. shows the following Balance Sheet (partial): Authorized Capital: 2,00,000 shares of ₹10 = ₹20,00,000. Called-up and Subscribed: 1,50,000 shares × ₹8 called = ₹12,00,000. Less Calls in Arrear: (₹60,000). Calls in Advance: shown under Current Liabilities = ₹30,000. What is the Paid-up Capital shown under Shareholders' Funds?
questions.explanation: Per Schedule III format for Notes to Accounts on Share Capital: Subscribed and Called-up = 1,50,000 × ₹8 = ₹12,00,000. Less: Calls in Arrear = (₹60,000). Paid-up Capital = ₹12,00,000 − ₹60,000 = ₹11,40,000. Note: Calls in Advance (₹30,000) is separately shown under Current Liabilities and is NOT part of Paid-up Capital. The face value per share on fully paid basis would be ₹10, but only ₹8 has been called. Paid-up Capital on Balance Sheet = ₹11,40,000. Wait: ₹12,00,000 − ₹60,000 = ₹11,40,000. Answer: C = ₹11,40,000.

questions.question: The par value of equity shares in India is typically one of which of the following denominations?
options: Only ₹100 per share as mandated by SEBI
Only ₹10 per share as mandated by Companies Act
₹1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10, ₹100 — companies can choose their face value
Any amount chosen by the Board of Directors freely
questions.explanation: Companies in India can choose the face (nominal/par) value of their shares from standard denominations. SEBI permits face values of ₹1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10, and ₹100 for listed companies. Many large companies have ₹1 or ₹2 face value shares (e.g., after sub-division). Unlisted companies have more flexibility but typically use standard values. There is no mandate for only ₹100 or ₹10. The choice of face value impacts the authorized capital calculation (number of shares × face value = authorized capital). Option D is wrong — SEBI guidelines constrain choices for listed companies.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A company can simultaneously have both 'Calls in Arrear' and 'Calls in Advance' on its Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Different shareholders may have different payment behaviors — some defaulting on calls (creating arrears) while others pay in advance (creating advance balance).
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. A is true — it is entirely possible for a company to simultaneously have Calls in Arrear (some shareholders haven't paid the called amount) and Calls in Advance (other shareholders have paid more than the called amount). R correctly explains the mechanism: different shareholders have different financial situations and motivations. Some may face financial difficulty (creating arrears), while others prefer to pay in advance (perhaps to earn 6% p.a. interest on advance). These are shareholder-specific behaviors tracked separately in company records and Balance Sheet.

questions.question: Serpentis Ltd. has issued 20,000 equity shares of ₹10 each. Shareholders holding more than 5% are: Mr. Bhatt (1,500 shares), Mrs. Jha (1,200 shares), XYZ Ltd. (5,000 shares). Which of these must be disclosed in Notes to Accounts?
options: Only XYZ Ltd. (25% holding)
Mr. Bhatt and XYZ Ltd.
All three shareholders
Only Mr. Bhatt and Mrs. Jha
questions.explanation: Disclosure threshold = more than 5% of total paid-up equity share capital. Total shares = 20,000. 5% threshold = 1,000 shares. Mr. Bhatt = 1,500/20,000 = 7.5% → Must disclose. Mrs. Jha = 1,200/20,000 = 6% → Must disclose. XYZ Ltd. = 5,000/20,000 = 25% → Must disclose. All three exceed the 5% threshold. Hence, ALL THREE must be disclosed in Notes to Accounts. Option A (only XYZ) is wrong. Option D (only Mr. Bhatt and Mrs. Jha) excludes XYZ. The correct answer is option C — all three, not just XYZ Ltd. which has 25%.

questions.question: Corvid Ltd. was incorporated with Authorized Capital of ₹1,00,00,000 (10,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each). In Year 1, it issued 5,00,000 shares at ₹12, all subscribed and fully paid. In Year 2, it issued 2,00,000 bonus shares from Securities Premium Reserve. In Year 3, it bought back 50,000 equity shares. What is the equity share capital after Year 3?
questions.explanation: Year 1: Issued 5,00,000 shares at ₹12. Share Capital = 5,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,00,000. SPR = 5,00,000 × ₹2 = ₹10,00,000. Year 2: Bonus Issue of 2,00,000 shares funded from SPR (2,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹20,00,000 > SPR of ₹10,00,000? Only ₹10,00,000 SPR available — so only 1,00,000 bonus shares possible from SPR. Assuming other sources cover rest: Share Capital after bonus = ₹50,00,000 + ₹20,00,000 = ₹70,00,000 (7,00,000 shares). Year 3: Buyback 50,000 shares at face = 50,000 × ₹10 = ₹5,00,000. Share Capital after buyback = ₹70,00,000 − ₹5,00,000 = ₹65,00,000.

questions.question: In which of the following situations does Subscribed Capital EQUAL Issued Capital?
options: When the company has uncalled capital
When the issue is exactly fully subscribed (neither over nor under subscribed)
When the company has calls in arrear
When the company has issued shares at premium
questions.explanation: Subscribed Capital = Issued Capital WHEN the company's share issue is exactly fully subscribed — every share issued has been applied for and allotted, with no excess applications (which would require rejection) and no deficiency (which would leave shares unsubscribed). In oversubscription, subscribed = issued (company allots up to the issued amount). In undersubscription, subscribed < issued. In exact full subscription, subscribed = issued. Options A, C, D relate to called-up/paid-up differences or premium issues — none of these affect the Subscribed vs. Issued relationship. Full subscription (or oversubscription) makes them equal.

questions.question: Volans Ltd. (a listed company) has 1,00,00,000 equity shares of ₹1 each. The company proposes to consolidate to ₹10 face value shares. After consolidation, the company further proposes a bonus issue of 1:10 (1 share for every 10 held). What will be the final number of equity shares and total equity share capital?
questions.explanation: Step 1 - Consolidation: 1,00,00,000 shares of ₹1 → 10,00,000 shares of ₹10 (÷ 10). Share Capital = 10,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹1,00,00,000 (unchanged). Step 2 - Bonus issue 1:10: 1 new share per 10 existing shares = 10,00,000/10 = 1,00,000 bonus shares of ₹10 each = ₹10,00,000 bonus. Total shares after bonus = 10,00,000 + 1,00,000 = 11,00,000 shares. Total share capital = ₹1,00,00,000 + ₹10,00,000 = ₹1,10,00,000. Answer: 11,00,000 shares and ₹1,10,00,000 total share capital = Option B.

questions.question: The capital clause in the Memorandum of Association of a company states the Authorized Capital as ₹50,00,000 divided into 5,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each. The company has issued 3,00,000 shares only. How much is the 'Unissued Capital'?
questions.explanation: Unissued Capital = Authorized Capital − Issued Capital. Authorized Capital = 5,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,00,000. Issued Capital = 3,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹30,00,000. Unissued Capital = ₹50,00,000 − ₹30,00,000 = ₹20,00,000. This represents the 2,00,000 shares (₹20,00,000) that the company has authority to issue (within its Authorized Capital) but has not yet offered to investors. The company can issue these shares in the future without needing to amend its MoA (as long as it stays within authorized limits).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Companies Act, 2013 requires that preference shares issued after its enactment cannot be irredeemable.
Reason (R): To protect investors and improve corporate governance, the 2013 Act mandates a maximum redemption period of 20 years for all preference shares (with special provisions for infrastructure companies).
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. Under Section 55 of the Companies Act, 2013, a company is NOT permitted to issue irredeemable preference shares — all preference shares must be redeemable within a period not exceeding 20 years from the date of issue (subject to Rules for infrastructure companies which may have longer periods). R correctly explains the reason and mechanism: the Act mandates a 20-year maximum redemption period, effectively making all new preference shares redeemable. This was a significant change from the Companies Act, 1956 which permitted irredeemable preference shares.

questions.question: Vulpecula Ltd. presents Notes to Accounts showing: Authorized: 5,00,000 shares × ₹10 = ₹50,00,000. Issued, Subscribed and Called-up: 4,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹40,00,000. Less: Calls in Arrear ₹(1,00,000). Total = ₹39,00,000. Calls in Advance = ₹50,000. A student adds Calls in Advance to get ₹39,50,000 as Share Capital. Is this correct?
options: Yes — Calls in Advance should be added to arrive at total funds from shareholders
No — Calls in Advance is a Current Liability and should NOT be added to Share Capital; paid-up capital remains ₹39,00,000
Yes — Calls in Advance represents shareholder funds and must be part of Share Capital
No — Calls in Advance should be deducted from Share Capital
questions.explanation: The student is WRONG. Calls in Advance (₹50,000) is a CURRENT LIABILITY — the company owes this amount to the shareholders who paid in advance (it will be adjusted against future calls, or returned if shares are forfeited). It is shown under 'Other Current Liabilities' on the Balance Sheet, NOT under Share Capital. The paid-up Share Capital = Called-up − Calls in Arrear = ₹40,00,000 − ₹1,00,000 = ₹39,00,000. Adding calls in advance to share capital would overstate equity and is a fundamental accounting error. Calls in advance is an obligation, not earned/called capital.

questions.question: Which of the following is an external source of long-term finance for a company?
options: Retained earnings
Issue of equity and preference shares to public
Depreciation provision
Transfer from General Reserve
questions.explanation: External sources of long-term finance involve raising funds from outside the company — from investors, lenders, or the public. Issue of equity and preference shares to the public is an external source as it brings in new capital from outside investors. Retained earnings (Option A), Depreciation provision (Option C), and Transfer from General Reserve (Option D) are all internal sources — they are generated within the company from its own operations and accumulated surpluses. External financing through share issue is the primary way companies raise long-term equity capital.

questions.question: Lacerta Ltd. has 9% Preference Shares of ₹1,00,000 (cumulative) with dividend 2 years in arrears. Equity Share Capital = ₹5,00,000. Profit available for distribution = ₹40,000. The Board pays preference first. What happens to equity?
options: Equity gets ₹13,000
Equity gets ₹0; preference gets ₹27,000 and ₹13,000 remains as preference arrears
Equity gets ₹4,000; preference gets ₹36,000
Equity gets ₹0; preference gets ₹40,000, ₹5,000 arrears remain
questions.explanation: Annual preference dividend = 9% × ₹1,00,000 = ₹9,000. 2 years arrears = ₹18,000. Current year preference = ₹9,000. Total preference due = ₹18,000 + ₹9,000 = ₹27,000. Available profit = ₹40,000. Since ₹40,000 > ₹27,000, preference is fully paid = ₹27,000. Balance for equity = ₹40,000 − ₹27,000 = ₹13,000. Equity gets ₹13,000. Wait — but this is ₹13,000 for equity out of ₹5,00,000 equity capital = 2.6% dividend. So Equity DOES get something: ₹13,000. Correct answer should be Option A: Equity gets ₹13,000. None of the options exactly match — Option A is closest.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT true about the Balance Sheet presentation of Share Capital as per Schedule III?
options: Authorized Share Capital is disclosed in Notes to Accounts with number and value of each class
Shares held by shareholders with more than 5% holding must be disclosed
The Securities Premium Reserve amount must be disclosed inside the Share Capital Note
Calls in Arrear is deducted from Called-up Capital to arrive at Paid-up Capital in Notes
questions.explanation: Option C is NOT true. Securities Premium Reserve is NOT disclosed inside the Share Capital Note — it is a separate item disclosed under 'Reserves and Surplus' in the Balance Sheet Notes (Note on Reserves and Surplus), NOT within the Share Capital note. The Share Capital note covers only: authorized capital, issued capital, subscribed capital (split into fully paid and not fully paid), reconciliation, rights/restrictions, related party holdings, and >5% shareholders. Securities Premium is a reserve, not share capital. Options A, B, and D are all correctly described mandatory disclosures within the Share Capital Notes.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Equity shares represent ownership interest and carry maximum residual risk among all securities issued by a company.
Reason (R): Equity shareholders receive income and capital repayment only after all other claimants (creditors, preference shareholders) have been satisfied.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. Equity shares represent OWNERSHIP — shareholders are the ultimate owners and residual claimants. They face maximum risk because they are last in the queue: (a) All creditors (secured, preferential, unsecured) are paid first; (b) Then preference shareholders (dividend arrears + capital); (c) Only then do equity shareholders receive anything. In bad years, equity shareholders may receive nothing; in great years, they may receive significant returns. R precisely explains WHY equity carries maximum residual risk — because of the payment hierarchy where equity comes last.

questions.question: Triangulum Ltd. has Authorized Capital of ₹2,00,00,000 (equity shares ₹1,00,00,000 + preference shares ₹1,00,00,000). It has issued: Equity shares: 8,00,000 × ₹10 = ₹80,00,000; 8% Preference Shares: 500 × ₹1,000 = ₹5,00,000. All subscribed and fully paid. Securities Premium (equity) = ₹16,00,000. Total Shareholders' Funds (assuming no other reserves) = ?
questions.explanation: Equity Share Capital = ₹80,00,000. Preference Share Capital = ₹5,00,000. Total Share Capital = ₹85,00,000. Reserves and Surplus = Securities Premium Reserve = ₹16,00,000. Total Shareholders' Funds = ₹85,00,000 + ₹16,00,000 = ₹1,01,00,000. Amount actually received from equity shareholders = 8,00,000 × (₹10 + ₹2 premium) = ₹96,00,000. Amount received from preference shareholders = ₹5,00,000. Total raised = ₹1,01,00,000 = Shareholders' Funds. This checks out. Answer: ₹1,01,00,000 (Option A).

questions.question: When comparing 'Equity Shares' with 'Debentures' as investment options for a risk-averse investor, which statement is MOST accurate?
options: Equity shares are better for risk-averse investors due to ownership rights
Debentures are preferred by risk-averse investors as they offer fixed interest and priority in repayment
Both are equally risky for investors
Equity shares are safer because shareholders have limited liability
questions.explanation: For risk-averse investors, debentures (debt instruments) are preferred because: (a) Fixed, contractually obligated interest regardless of profit/loss, (b) Priority in repayment over ALL shareholders at maturity and in winding up, (c) Secured debentures have asset backing. Equity shares carry variable dividend, no guaranteed return, and residual claim — unsuitable for risk-averse investors. Option A is wrong — ownership rights don't reduce investment risk. Option C is factually wrong — they have very different risk profiles. Option D confuses shareholders' limited liability (protection from company debts) with investment risk (which is higher for equity).

questions.question: Puppis Ltd. filed its Balance Sheet with the following Share Capital Note: Authorized: ₹20,00,000 (2,00,000 equity shares × ₹10). Issued: ₹15,00,000 (1,50,000 shares × ₹10 at ₹13 each). Subscribed and Fully Paid: 1,50,000 × ₹10 = ₹15,00,000. The auditor says the Note is incomplete. What is missing?
options: The number of authorized shares must be in words
Securities Premium of ₹4,50,000 (1,50,000 × ₹3) must be mentioned in the Share Capital Note
The reconciliation of shares at beginning and end of year and rights/preferences attached to shares must be disclosed
The Note is complete — no information is missing
questions.explanation: The auditor is correct. Per Schedule III requirements for Share Capital Notes, the following must be disclosed but are MISSING: (a) Reconciliation of shares outstanding at the beginning and end of the period; (b) Rights, preferences and restrictions attached to each class of shares; (c) Shares held by holding/ultimate holding company; (d) Details of shareholders holding >5%; (e) History of shares issued as bonus/buyback in last 5 years. Option B: Securities Premium is NOT part of the Share Capital Note — it belongs to the Reserves and Surplus note. The reconciliation and rights disclosures are missing.

questions.question: A company converts 10,000 fully paid equity shares of ₹10 each into stock. Subsequently, the company issues stock of ₹25,000 to a creditor in settlement of debt. How does this affect the Share Capital account?
options: Share Capital decreases by ₹25,000 since stock is issued to a non-shareholder
No effect — stock is just another form of equity and conversion/issue at face value doesn't change total share capital
Share Capital increases by ₹25,000 as new stock is issued
Share Capital reduces by ₹10,000 as 1,000 shares are converted
questions.explanation: When shares are converted to stock, the total Share Capital doesn't change — it's just a change in form. The conversion of 10,000 shares × ₹10 to ₹1,00,000 worth of stock = same amount. If ₹25,000 of stock is ISSUED to a creditor: this is a new issuance (not from existing converted stock). New stock issued = new capital raised. Share Capital increases by ₹25,000 (the face value of stock issued). Actually — if ₹25,000 is issued from the EXISTING converted stock (transferring existing stock as payment), that's a settlement without creating new capital. But 'issuing' stock to a creditor typically creates new capital. Context matters; option B is most conservative answer.

questions.question: Apus Ltd. has Authorized Capital of ₹1,00,00,000. Issued: 6,00,000 equity shares at ₹10 (par). Applications: 8,00,000 shares at ₹2 on application. Company allots 6,00,000 on pro-rata, excess ₹2 refunded. Allotment money = ₹5 per share. First call = ₹2 per share. All paid on allotment; 10,000 shares defaulted on first call. Calculate Calls in Arrear after First Call.
questions.explanation: First Call = ₹2 per share. Shares on which first call defaulted = 10,000. Calls in Arrear = 10,000 × ₹2 = ₹20,000. All other shareholders (6,00,000 − 10,000 = 5,90,000) paid. Calls in Arrear = ₹20,000. The earlier pro-rata allotment and application refund do not affect Calls in Arrear calculation — that is straightforward: defaulted shares × call amount = ₹20,000. This ₹20,000 is shown as deduction from Called-up Capital in the Share Capital Notes. Answer: Option A = ₹20,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about the 'Winding Up' of a company in relation to share capital?
options: Share capital is repaid first before creditors to protect shareholders
Equity shareholders receive capital repayment only if assets are sufficient after paying all liabilities and preference shareholders
All shareholders receive equal repayment regardless of share type
Share capital repayment equals face value always, regardless of remaining assets
questions.explanation: In winding up, the correct sequence is: First, all liabilities (secured creditors, preferential creditors, unsecured creditors) are settled. Then, preference shareholders receive repayment of their capital (plus any dividend arrears). Finally, equity shareholders receive whatever remains — which could be more than face value (if assets > liabilities + preference claims) or less (or nothing, in case of insufficient assets). Option A reverses the priority — shareholders are paid after creditors, not before. Option C ignores preference/equity distinction. Option D is wrong — equity may receive less than face value if assets are insufficient.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Paid-up Share Capital is always less than or equal to Called-up Capital.
Reason (R): Some shareholders may not pay the full called amount (calls in arrear), reducing the actual cash received below the called amount, but no shareholder can pay MORE than what's been called and have it counted in paid-up capital.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is TRUE — Paid-up Capital ≤ Called-up Capital (because of calls in arrear). However, Reason R is FALSE in its second part. Shareholders CAN and DO pay more than what's been called — this is Calls in Advance. When shareholders pay in advance, it is credited to 'Calls in Advance' (a Current Liability), NOT to Paid-up Capital. So R is incorrect in saying advance payments 'count in paid-up capital' — they don't. Paid-up Capital specifically reflects only the called amount received; advance payments are treated separately. Hence A is true (Paid-up ≤ Called-up) but R's explanation is flawed.

questions.question: Tucana Ltd. has equity share capital of ₹1,00,00,000 (1,00,00,000 shares × ₹1 each — ₹1 face value). The company's share trades at ₹200 on BSE. A new investor calculates: 'Market Cap = ₹200 crore; Share Capital on Balance Sheet = ₹1 crore — so Balance Sheet understates value by ₹199 crore.' Identify what the investor has confused.
options: Investor is correct — Balance Sheet should show market value
Investor has confused Face Value (₹1) with Market Value (₹200) — Share Capital is recorded at face value per accounting standards; market value is not recorded on Balance Sheet
Investor is wrong because market cap should use book value, not market price
Investor is right — Securities Premium should be ₹199 crore on Balance Sheet
questions.explanation: The investor has made a fundamental confusion between Face Value and Market Value. Accounting standards require Share Capital to be recorded at FACE VALUE (₹1 per share here), not market value. The ₹200 market price includes: (a) original premium at issue, (b) accumulated retained earnings, (c) future growth expectations, (d) market sentiment. Balance Sheet shows historical cost/accounting values, not current market values (except for certain instruments under fair value accounting). Securities Premium (₹199 per share) cannot be retroactively created just because the market values the company highly — SPR is only created at the time of issue above face value.

questions.question: Pictor Ltd. shows these figures: Share Capital (all fully paid) ₹50,00,000; General Reserve ₹10,00,000; Securities Premium ₹5,00,000; P&L Surplus ₹8,00,000; Long-term Borrowings ₹15,00,000; Current Liabilities ₹12,00,000. What is the Debt-to-Equity ratio?
questions.explanation: Debt = Long-term Borrowings = ₹15,00,000 (typically, current liabilities excluded from traditional D/E ratio). Shareholders' Equity = Share Capital + Reserves = ₹50,00,000 + ₹10,00,000 + ₹5,00,000 + ₹8,00,000 = ₹73,00,000. Wait: if total debt includes current liabilities too: Total Debt = ₹15,00,000 + ₹12,00,000 = ₹27,00,000. D/E = ₹27,00,000 / ₹73,00,000 = 0.37. With only LTB: D/E = ₹15,00,000 / ₹73,00,000 = 0.205 ≈ 0.21 (Option D). With current liabilities included: 0.37. None match perfectly. If Equity = ₹50,00,000 only: D/E = ₹15,00,000/₹50,00,000 = 0.30. Option B (0.57) = ₹15,00,000 + ₹12,00,000 = ₹27,00,000 / ₹47,00,000. Various interpretations. Most standard = Option B.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about 'Share Capital' and 'Loan Capital' on the Balance Sheet is CORRECT?
options: Both Share Capital and Debentures appear under 'Shareholders' Funds'
Share Capital appears under Shareholders' Funds; Debentures appear under Non-Current Liabilities
Share Capital appears under Current Liabilities; Debentures under Non-Current Assets
Both appear under Non-Current Liabilities
questions.explanation: Under Schedule III (Companies Act 2013): Share Capital appears under 'Shareholders' Funds' (under Equity and Liabilities) as it represents ownership capital. Debentures (Loan Capital) appear under 'Non-Current Liabilities' → Long-term Borrowings (if redeemable after 12 months). Debentures due within 12 months would appear under Current Liabilities (current maturities). This distinction is important: Shareholders' Funds = owned capital; Long-term Borrowings = borrowed capital. They have fundamentally different risk profiles, rights, and obligations, which is why they are classified separately on the Balance Sheet.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The total amount shown for Share Capital in the Balance Sheet of a company equals the amount actually received from shareholders.
Reason (R): Share Capital is always fully paid up in modern companies, as SEBI and the Companies Act prohibit issuance of shares without full payment.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false and R is also false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are FALSE. Assertion A is false: The Share Capital shown on the Balance Sheet is the PAID-UP Capital (= Called-up − Calls in Arrear), which equals the amount actually received. However, this may be LESS than the full face value of issued shares (because of uncalled capital and calls in arrear). The subscribed capital at face value may exceed the paid-up amount. Reason R is false: Companies can issue shares by making calls in installments — shares need not be fully paid immediately. Companies Act and SEBI permit installment payments. Many companies issue shares where only part of the face value is called initially. Both statements are incorrect.

questions.question: Mensa Ltd.'s annual report shows: Authorized Capital ₹1,00,00,000; Issued Capital ₹60,00,000; Subscribed & Fully Paid ₹55,00,000; Subscribed not fully paid: 5,000 shares × ₹100 face, ₹60 called, ₹50 paid. Calls in Advance = ₹10,000. Prepare the total Share Capital for the Notes to Accounts and identify where Calls in Advance will appear.
options: Share Capital Note total = ₹55,00,000; Calls in Advance under Shareholders' Funds
Share Capital Note total = ₹57,50,000; Calls in Advance under Current Liabilities
Share Capital Note total = ₹57,50,000 with Calls in Arrear deducted; Calls in Advance under Other Current Liabilities
Share Capital Note total = ₹57,50,000; Calls in Advance deducted from Share Capital
questions.explanation: Subscribed & Fully Paid = ₹55,00,000. Subscribed but not fully paid (called amount) = 5,000 × ₹60 = ₹3,00,000. Calls in Arrear = 5,000 × (₹60−₹50) = 5,000 × ₹10 = ₹50,000. Share Capital Note: ₹55,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 = ₹58,00,000 (called amount). Less Calls in Arrear: (₹50,000). Net Paid-up = ₹57,50,000. Calls in Advance (₹10,000) → shown separately under 'Other Current Liabilities' on Balance Sheet face — NOT deducted from or added to Share Capital. Option C correctly states the total (₹57,50,000 after deducting calls in arrear) and the proper placement of Calls in Advance. Answer: C.
